walkthrough.title: Assemble the Ocean Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get this icy playground ready! Grab the four ocean boards from the box. These are the puzzle-piece shaped boards that interlock to form the playing area. Snap them together so that the fish icons on each board are all facing the same direction. You should end up with a big rectangular ocean frame with empty hex spaces inside, waiting to be filled with ice.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Ice Floe Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now find all 60 hexagonal ice floe tiles. These are the heart of the game. Flip them all face-down so you can see the water side, and give them a good shuffle. Mix them up thoroughly so nobody knows where the juicy three-fish tiles will end up. There are 30 one-fish tiles, 20 two-fish tiles, and 10 three-fish tiles in the mix.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Board with Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now take all 60 shuffled tiles and place them face-up (fish side showing) onto every hex space on the ocean boards. Fill every single space — no gaps allowed. The result should be a big, beautiful ice field covered in fish. If your group agrees, you can swap some tiles around to make sure the two-fish and three-fish tiles are spread more evenly across the board. But this is totally optional.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Penguin Color
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color. There are four penguin colors in the box, each with four penguin figures. It does not matter which color you choose — the penguins are all equally adorable and equally hungry for fish.

walkthrough.title: Grab Your Penguins by Player Count
walkthrough.text: Here is where player count matters. With 2 players, each person takes 4 penguins. With 3 players, each person takes 3 penguins. With 4 players, each person takes 2 penguins. More players means fewer penguins each, which keeps the board from getting too crowded and makes every placement decision count.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Penguins
walkthrough.text: Starting with the youngest player and going clockwise, each player places one penguin at a time onto any unoccupied ice floe tile that shows exactly one fish. That is the key rule — penguins can only be placed on one-fish tiles during setup. Keep going around the table, one penguin at a time, until everyone has placed all their penguins. This means placement is strategic even before the game starts!

walkthrough.title: Why Only One-Fish Tiles?
walkthrough.text: You might be wondering why penguins can only start on one-fish tiles. It keeps the opening fair — nobody gets to plop down on a juicy three-fish tile right away. Instead, you have to think about where your penguin will move on its first turn. Look at the tiles surrounding your placement spot. Are there high-value tiles nearby? Are you close to a cluster of fish, or stuck in a dead end? That is the real strategy of placement.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play!
walkthrough.text: Once every penguin is standing on the ice, you are good to go. The board should look like a colorful field of hexagonal tiles with penguins scattered across it, each one eyeing the fish around them. The youngest player will take the first turn. Let's learn how to play!

bullets: Place all 60 tiles face-up on every hex space — no gaps
Optional: swap tiles by group agreement for even distribution

summary.title: Choose Your Penguin Color
bullets: 4 colors available, each with 4 penguin figures
Each player picks one color

summary.title: Grab Your Penguins by Player Count

3 players: 3 penguins each
4 players: 2 penguins each

summary.title: Place Your Penguins

Tile must be unoccupied — continue until all penguins are placed

summary.title: Why Only One-Fish Tiles?

Placement strategy: look at surrounding tiles for future moves

summary.title: Ready to Play!
bullets: All penguins placed on one-fish tiles across the board
Youngest player takes the first turn

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Each turn is beautifully simple. You do two things, always in this order: first, move one of your penguins, and then collect the ice floe tile that penguin just left behind. That is it. Move a penguin, pick up a tile. The game flows clockwise from the youngest player, one turn at a time.

walkthrough.title: Moving Your Penguin
walkthrough.text: Pick any one of your penguins to move. Your penguin can slide in a straight line as far as you want in any of the six directions a hexagon allows. It can move one space or zoom across the whole board — your choice. But there is one critical rule: your penguin cannot change direction during a move. Pick a direction and commit to it.

walkthrough.title: What Blocks Movement
walkthrough.text: Your penguin must stop before hitting any obstacle. It cannot move onto or through a tile occupied by another penguin — even your own penguin. It also cannot cross an empty space where a tile has already been collected. Think of those gaps as open water. Penguins are great swimmers in real life, but in this game, they cannot cross the water. If a penguin is completely surrounded by other penguins and empty spaces, it is stuck.

walkthrough.title: Collecting the Tile
walkthrough.text: After you move your penguin, pick up the tile your penguin was standing on before the move — that is, the tile it just left. Add this tile to your personal collection, face-up in a pile in front of you. The fish printed on that tile are your points. Pro tip from the rulebook: push on one edge of the tile to pop it out easily without disturbing the neighbors.

walkthrough.title: The Board Shrinks
walkthrough.text: Here is what makes the game so deliciously tense. Every time someone moves, a tile disappears from the board. The ice field gets smaller and smaller each turn, creating gaps that block movement. Paths that were open a moment ago might suddenly be cut off. The board literally crumbles around you. This is the core of the game — the shrinking board forces harder and harder decisions as the game goes on.

walkthrough.title: You Must Move
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must move one of your penguins if you can. You cannot pass or skip your turn. If at least one of your penguins has a legal move — meaning there is at least one adjacent, unoccupied tile it can reach — you have to move. You get to choose which penguin to move, but you must move something.

walkthrough.title: Getting Stranded
walkthrough.text: If none of your penguins can move — they are all completely surrounded by gaps and other penguins — you are out of the game. But do not worry, you do not leave empty-handed. Remove all your stranded penguins from the board, and collect the tiles they were standing on. Those tiles and their fish count toward your score. Play continues with the remaining players.

walkthrough.title: End of the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends when no penguin on the board has any legal moves left. All remaining penguins are removed and their tiles collected by their owners. Any uncollected tiles left on the board go back to the box — nobody gets those fish. Now count up the fish on all your collected tiles. The player with the most fish wins! If there is a tie, the tied player with the most collected tiles wins. Still tied? You share the victory. Not bad for a day on the ice.

summary.title: Turn Overview
bullets: Two steps per turn: 1) Move a penguin, 2) Collect the tile it left
Play goes clockwise from the youngest player

Cannot change direction mid-move

bullets: Cannot move onto or through a tile with any penguin (even your own)
Cannot cross empty spaces where tiles have been removed

summary.title: Collecting the Tile

Fish on collected tiles = your points

summary.title: The Board Shrinks
bullets: Every move removes a tile, creating gaps

The shrinking board is the core tension of the game

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Ice!
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted game with three players: Red, Blue, and Green. Each player has 3 penguins (the standard 3-player setup). The board is set up with all 60 tiles placed randomly. We will play through the first several turns so you can see movement, tile collection, and common mistakes in action. Ready? Let's waddle!

walkthrough.title: Penguin Placement Phase
walkthrough.text: Red is the youngest, so Red places first. Red puts a penguin on a one-fish tile near the center of the board — good positioning with lots of movement options. Blue places a penguin on a one-fish tile near a cluster of two-fish and three-fish tiles. Smart thinking ahead. Green places on a one-fish tile along one edge. They continue placing one at a time, clockwise, until all 9 penguins are on the board.

walkthrough.title: Red's First Turn: A Simple Move
walkthrough.text: Red looks at the board and picks a penguin near a three-fish tile. Red moves that penguin one space onto the three-fish tile. Now Red picks up the one-fish tile the penguin just left — that is one fish in Red's collection. The penguin is now sitting on the three-fish tile, and there is a gap where the one-fish tile used to be. Simple as that!

walkthrough.title: Blue's First Turn: Moving Multiple Spaces
walkthrough.text: Blue decides to make a longer move. Blue picks a penguin and slides it three spaces in a straight line, passing over two tiles to land on a two-fish tile further away. Remember, the penguin does not collect the tiles it passes over — only the tile it left behind. Blue picks up the starting one-fish tile. The tiles in between stay on the board, but the gap is left behind Blue's penguin.

walkthrough.title: Green's First Turn and a Common Mistake
walkthrough.text: Green wants to reach a three-fish tile across the board. Green tries to move a penguin two spaces to the right, then one space up to reach it. Hold on — that is an illegal move! A penguin must travel in a single straight line. It cannot change direction during a move. Green needs to pick one of the six hex directions and stick with it. Green reconsiders and moves the penguin in a straight line toward a two-fish tile instead. Legal move!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Straight Lines Only
walkthrough.text: This is the most common mistake new players make. Hexagons have six directions, and your penguin can go as far as it wants in any one of them, but it absolutely cannot zigzag or turn during a single move. If you cannot reach a tile in a straight line, you cannot get there this turn. Plan your moves by looking at the six straight paths from your penguin's current position.

walkthrough.title: Red's Second Turn: Blocking
walkthrough.text: Red notices that Blue's penguin is eyeing a cluster of three-fish tiles. Red moves a different penguin into a tile that sits between Blue's penguin and that cluster. Now Blue's path is blocked — Blue cannot move through Red's penguin. This is a totally legal and very strategic play. Blocking opponents is a huge part of this game.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Second Turn: Adjusting to the Block
walkthrough.text: Blue is blocked from the three-fish cluster, so Blue has to find another path. Blue moves a penguin in a different direction, picking up a two-fish tile. Not as lucrative as the three-fish tiles, but Blue is already thinking about positioning for next turn. The board is starting to develop gaps now, and every move changes the landscape.

walkthrough.title: Green's Second Turn: Forgetting to Collect
walkthrough.text: Green moves a penguin to a nice two-fish tile. Great move! But then Green forgets to pick up the tile the penguin left behind. The other players remind Green — you always collect the tile you moved FROM, not the tile you moved TO. Green grabs the one-fish tile from the starting position. Do not forget this step! The tile you land on stays on the board. The tile you leave gets collected.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: You Collect the Departure Tile
walkthrough.text: It is easy to get confused about which tile you collect, especially when you are excited about landing on a high-value tile. Remember: the tile under your penguin is not scored yet. You score it when you move away from it on a future turn, or when your penguin gets stranded there at the end. So landing on a three-fish tile does not give you three fish right now — it gives you three fish later when you leave.

walkthrough.title: The Board Is Shrinking
walkthrough.text: After just two turns each, there are already six gaps in the ice. Some tiles that were connected are now becoming isolated. Red's blocking move earlier cut off a section of the board. Notice how the game already feels different — paths are closing, options are narrowing. This is the beauty of the game. Every single move changes the geography for everyone.

walkthrough.title: Red Claims an Island
walkthrough.text: Red makes a clever play. Red moves a penguin so that it ends up on a small peninsula of three tiles, and the tile Red collects from the move creates a gap that cuts this peninsula off from the rest of the board. Now Red's penguin is alone on a little island with two tiles to collect, and nobody else can get there. Red has guaranteed those fish for themselves!

walkthrough.title: Green Tries an Illegal Move Through a Gap
walkthrough.text: Green wants to move a penguin across the board, but there is an empty space in the path where a tile was already collected. Green tries to jump over the gap. Nope! Penguins cannot cross empty spaces. The gap might as well be open ocean. Green has to find a different direction that has an unbroken line of tiles.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Gaps Are Walls
walkthrough.text: Every removed tile becomes an impassable gap. Your penguin cannot hop over, swim through, or teleport past an empty space. This is exactly what makes blocking and island-creation so powerful. When you remove a tile, you are not just scoring fish — you are building walls on the board. Think about how each move reshapes the terrain for everyone.

walkthrough.title: Blue Gets Stranded
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, one of Blue's penguins is stuck. It is completely surrounded by gaps and other penguins with no legal move in any direction. Blue does not lose this penguin's tile though — Blue collects the tile under the stranded penguin. That fish still counts! Blue's other penguins keep playing normally. Only the stranded penguin is removed.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Push
walkthrough.text: As the board gets smaller, everyone is racing to grab the remaining high-value tiles before they get cut off. Red is systematically collecting from the island. Blue is sweeping along a row of tiles. Green is trying to block Blue while collecting two-fish tiles. The tension ratchets up because every move matters more now — one wrong move and you could strand your own penguin on a single one-fish tile.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when no penguin can move. Everyone collects the tiles their stranded penguins were sitting on. Uncollected tiles go back to the box. Time to count! Red collected 14 tiles worth 28 fish. Blue collected 12 tiles worth 24 fish. Green collected 11 tiles worth 21 fish. Red wins! The island play and early blocking paid off big time.

walkthrough.title: Before You Hit the Ice
walkthrough.text:
You have seen movement, tile collection, blocking, island creation, and all three common mistakes — illegal direction changes, forgetting to collect tiles, and trying to cross gaps. You are ready to play for real!

First — check out General Tips for positioning strategy and board control.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick-reference of tile types and movement rules.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun out there on the ice!

summary.title: Before You Hit the Ice
bullets: Check out General Tips for positioning and board control strategy
Reference the Appendix for tile types and movement rules
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Placement Is Your First Move
walkthrough.text: The game begins before anyone takes a turn. When placing your penguins during setup, do not just plop them down randomly. Look at the tiles around each placement spot. You want your penguins near clusters of two-fish and three-fish tiles that they can reach in one or two moves. Avoid edges and corners where your movement options are limited. Central positions give you the most flexibility. Also, spread your penguins out — if they are all bunched together, they will block each other.

walkthrough.title: Think About the Tile You Leave, Not Just Where You Go
walkthrough.text: New players focus on where they are moving to, but experienced players think about what they are leaving behind. The tile you collect is the one you depart from, and the gap you create changes the board for everyone. Sometimes it is better to move away from a low-value tile (collecting it now) to position yourself for several high-value tiles later. Think two or three moves ahead.

walkthrough.title: Create Islands for Yourself
walkthrough.text: The strongest play in the game is isolating your penguin on a section of tiles that no other penguin can reach. When you do this, you can leisurely collect every tile in that section without competition. Look for opportunities to break off a peninsula by collecting a key tile that separates a group of tiles from the main board. Even a small island of four or five tiles can be worth more than fighting over contested territory.

walkthrough.title: Block Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Moving a penguin into an opponent's path is one of the most powerful things you can do. If you see an opponent lining up to grab a three-fish tile, consider parking one of your penguins in their way. This forces them to take a less valuable route. Be careful though — if you spend too much effort blocking, you might neglect your own fish collection. The best blocks are ones that also put you in a good position.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Strand Yourself on Bad Tiles
walkthrough.text: As the board shrinks, it is tempting to grab every fish you can. But watch out for moves that leave your penguin stranded on a single one-fish tile. That penguin is done, and it only collected one fish. Instead, try to keep at least two or three tiles around each of your penguins so they have room to maneuver. Sometimes it is better to take a less valuable tile this turn if it means keeping your penguin alive and scoring for several more turns.

walkthrough.title: Count the Fish
walkthrough.text: This sounds obvious, but keep a rough count of how many fish everyone has collected. If you are ahead, play conservatively — protect your penguins and avoid risky moves. If you are behind, you need to be aggressive — go for three-fish tiles and try to cut off opponents from high-value areas. Knowing the score helps you make better decisions in the endgame when every single fish matters.

walkthrough.title: Use All Your Penguins
walkthrough.text: Do not favor one penguin and ignore the others. Each penguin is a tool for collecting fish and shaping the board. Sometimes you should move a penguin not because it collects a great tile, but because the gap it creates benefits your other penguins. Think of your penguins as a team — move the one that creates the best overall position, even if it is not the one sitting next to the fattest fish.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to Player Count
walkthrough.text: The game feels very different with two players versus four. With two players and four penguins each, you have a lot of control over the board, and it is easier to create islands and execute long-term plans. With four players and just two penguins each, the board is chaotic and changes dramatically between your turns. Adapt your strategy: with fewer penguins, focus on immediate gains; with more penguins, plan further ahead.

summary.title: Adapt to Player Count

Adjust strategy based on how much the board changes between turns

walkthrough.title: The Island Strategy
walkthrough.text: The single most powerful strategy in Hey, That's My Fish is engineering islands — isolated sections of tiles accessible only to your penguins. During placement, position your penguins near natural chokepoints where the board narrows to one or two tiles wide. In your early moves, aim to collect the tiles that act as bridges to those sections. Once you sever the connection, you have a private fish reserve. Advanced players can plan island creation from their very first placement.

walkthrough.title: Chokepoint Control
walkthrough.text: A chokepoint is a narrow connection between two larger sections of the board. These form naturally as tiles are collected, but you can accelerate the process. If you see two clusters of tiles connected by a single row of one or two tiles, position a penguin to control that bridge. When you collect the bridge tile, you cut the board in two. If your penguin is on the right side, you get all those fish. This is why central placement is so strong — it gives you access to more chokepoints.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Fish Per Move
walkthrough.text: Every turn, you collect exactly one tile. That means the total number of turns you take is the total number of tiles you collect. To maximize your score, you want every tile you collect to be as valuable as possible. This means avoiding one-fish tiles whenever you can (after setup) and steering your penguins toward two-fish and three-fish tiles. When you have a choice between two equally good positions, always choose the one that leaves you collecting higher-value tiles.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Denial
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best move is not the one that gives you the most fish, but the one that takes the most fish away from your opponent. If an opponent has a clear path to a row of three-fish tiles, inserting your penguin into that path is devastating. They lose access to several high-value tiles, and you gain a good position. In a tight game, denying three fish to your opponent is as powerful as collecting three fish yourself. Always scan the board for opponent plans before deciding your move.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Penguin Sacrifice
walkthrough.text: Here is a counter-intuitive advanced play: sometimes you should intentionally strand one of your penguins. If a penguin is sitting on a three-fish tile but has nowhere great to go — maybe all surrounding tiles are one-fish — it might be better to use your turn on a different penguin that has a more productive path. The stranded penguin still collects the tile it is sitting on at game end. You sacrifice that penguin's future moves, but if its only options were one-fish tiles, you are not losing much.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board
walkthrough.text: Advanced players see the board differently. Instead of looking at individual tiles, they see corridors — unbroken lines of tiles a penguin can travel. They count the total fish value along each corridor. They spot which corridors overlap with opponents' possible moves. Before making any move, scan the board for: Which corridors offer the highest total fish? Which corridors are contested? Where are the chokepoints forming? This bird's-eye analysis takes practice but dramatically improves your play.

walkthrough.title: Placement Mind Games
walkthrough.text: In a game with experienced players, penguin placement becomes a psychological battle. If you place near a high-value area, opponents may place nearby to contest it. Consider placing your first penguin in a seemingly weak position to lure opponents away from your real target, then place your later penguins where you actually want them. In a 2-player game with four placements each, the last few placements are especially powerful since you know exactly where your opponent's penguins are.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Calculation
walkthrough.text: When the board is down to its last 15-20 tiles, the game shifts from intuition to pure calculation. Count exactly how many tiles each of your penguins can collect before getting stranded. Count the total fish. Then do the same for your opponents. If you are ahead, play safe and collect your guaranteed fish. If you are behind, look for aggressive moves that change the board — break an opponent's island, block their most productive penguin, or race to grab a contested three-fish tile before they can. The player who calculates the endgame most accurately almost always wins.

summary.title: The Island Strategy
bullets: Engineer isolated sections accessible only to your penguins
Position near natural chokepoints during placement
Sever the bridge tile to create a private fish reserve
Plan island creation from your very first placement

summary.title: Chokepoint Control
bullets: Chokepoints: narrow 1-2 tile connections between larger sections
Position a penguin to control the bridge, then collect it to split the board
Central placement gives access to the most chokepoints

summary.title: Maximizing Fish Per Move
bullets: You collect exactly 1 tile per turn — make each one count
Steer toward 2- and 3-fish tiles after setup
When positions are equal, choose the path with higher-value tiles

summary.title: Opponent Denial
bullets: Blocking an opponent from 3 fish = as valuable as collecting 3 fish
Insert your penguin into opponents' high-value paths
Always scan for opponent plans before choosing your move

summary.title: Late-Game Penguin Sacrifice
bullets: Sometimes stranding a penguin on a high-value tile is correct
If its only moves are to 1-fish tiles, use your turn on another penguin
Stranded penguin still collects the tile it sits on at game end

summary.title: Reading the Board

Count total fish value along each corridor

bullets: Experienced opponents will contest your placement areas
Consider decoy placements to lure opponents away from your real target
In 2-player games, late placements are most powerful

summary.title: The Endgame Calculation
bullets: At 15-20 tiles remaining, shift from intuition to pure calculation
Count exact tiles and fish each penguin can collect
Ahead? Play safe. Behind? Play aggressive and disrupt opponents
Best endgame calculator almost always wins

entries.definition: An ice floe tile showing a single fish. There are 30 of these in the game — the most common tile type. Penguins must be placed on one-fish tiles during setup. Worth 1 point when collected.
An ice floe tile showing two fish. There are 20 of these in the game. Worth 2 points when collected. A solid mid-value target throughout the game.
An ice floe tile showing three fish. There are only 10 of these — the rarest and most valuable tile type. Worth 3 points when collected. High-priority targets for all players.

One of four interlocking frame pieces that form the playing area. The boards snap together to create a rectangular grid of hex spaces. Fish icons on the boards must all face the same direction during assembly.

A small plastic penguin used to move across the board and claim tiles. Each color has 4 figures, though players may not use all 4 depending on player count. Penguins block movement for all players, including their owner.

The fundamental movement rule: a penguin must move in exactly one of the six hexagonal directions and cannot change direction during the move. The penguin may travel any number of unoccupied, unbroken tiles in that direction.
A penguin cannot move onto or through a tile occupied by another penguin, including its own penguins. The moving penguin must stop before any occupied tile.
A hex space where an ice floe tile has been collected, leaving open water. Penguins cannot move onto or across empty spaces. Gaps are permanent and reshape the board for the rest of the game.
On a hex grid, each tile connects to six neighbors. A penguin can move in any of these six directions on its turn, but must choose only one direction per move.

After moving a penguin, the player collects the ice floe tile the penguin departed from (not the tile it landed on). The collected tile is placed face-up in the player's personal pile. The fish on collected tiles determine the final score.

A penguin that has no legal moves because all adjacent tiles are occupied by other penguins or are empty gaps. When all of a player's penguins are stranded, that player is eliminated. The stranded penguins are removed and their tiles are collected by the owning player.
A player must move one of their penguins each turn if any legal move exists. You cannot voluntarily pass or skip your turn. If no legal moves remain for any of your penguins, you are eliminated.

A section of tiles that is completely disconnected from the rest of the board by gaps. If only one player's penguin is on an island, that player can collect all tiles in that section without interference. Creating islands is a key advanced strategy.
A narrow connection (one or two tiles wide) between two larger sections of the board. Controlling or collecting a chokepoint tile can split the board, trapping opponents or creating private islands.

Intentionally moving a penguin into a position that prevents an opponent from reaching high-value tiles. Since penguins cannot pass through each other, a well-placed penguin can cut off an opponent's path completely.
With 2 players, each player uses 4 penguins. This gives each player maximum control over the board and allows for more strategic, long-term planning.
With 3 players, each player uses 3 penguins. Considered by many the best player count, balancing control with competition. The recommended player count.
With 4 players, each player uses 2 penguins. The most chaotic setup — the board changes dramatically between your turns, requiring more tactical, in-the-moment play.
The youngest player at the table starts the game. They place the first penguin during setup and take the first turn during gameplay. Play proceeds clockwise from this player.

During setup, after placing all 60 tiles randomly, players may unanimously agree to swap tiles so that two-fish and three-fish tiles are more evenly distributed across the board. This is optional and must be agreed upon by all players.
If two or more players have the same number of fish at game end, the tied player with the most collected ice floe tiles wins. If still tied, those players share the victory.
The icons printed on ice floe tiles (1, 2, or 3 per tile). The total number of fish on all tiles a player has collected is their final score. There are 120 total fish across all 60 tiles.

The game ends when no penguin on the board has any legal move. All remaining penguins are removed, their tiles collected. Uncollected tiles return to the box. Players then count fish to determine the winner.
An unbroken line of tiles that a penguin can travel along in a single move. Advanced players evaluate corridors to find the highest-value paths and identify which ones are contested by opponents.
